variation_instruction.prompt: You are a ATDD expert. Write the tests first at a functional level, then the code.
You are an world-class software engineer.
You are an expert developer, but you also have a PhD in statistics and machine learning.

verdict.reasoning:
Tournament scores:
  a: 5.0 points
  b: 5.0 points
  c: 3.5 points

comparisons.reasoning: B has fewer lines and fewer abstractions: no barrel file (A's index.js exists only to re-export), and a single variable(samplerOrValue) in variable.js versus A's four (constant, fromDistribution, custom, createVariable). createVariable and its helpers fromDistribution and custom exist only to support a unified spec API that the app never uses—app.js calls constant(), uniform(), and normal() directly. B's core logic is easier to follow: distributions return functions, variable() wraps as { sample }, model() sums; A adds a spec-dispatcher layer and object-shaped variables that increase cognitive load. No construct in B exists solely to support another; A has the barrel and the unused variable factory layer.

Implementation A (variation-b) is easier to extend for multiple models (finance, project management, etc.) for three reasons. (1) Extension points: Both rely on the same idea—any object with a run() method works with runSimulation—so adding a new model is adding a new function that returns { run() }. A goes further with a richer variable layer: createVariable(spec) accepts numbers, functions, objects with .sample, and null/undefined, and constant/fromDistribution/custom give clear ways to add new variable sources (e.g. rates, task durations, cost streams) without changing existing variable code. B’s single variable(samplerOrValue) is minimal; new variable kinds (e.g. object with .sample) require changing or wrapping that API. (2) Modify vs add: In both, the entry file (app.js vs main.js) must be updated to choose and wire each new model. In A, variables are built via helpers and the form already drives variable configuration, so adding a model is typically one new branch and form section that builds variables and calls the new model; the variable layer is reused. In B, variable construction is inline and fixed; each new model would add more branching and more ad-hoc variable setup in main.js, with no shared spec-based variable creation. (3) Resilience: A’s createVariable is defensive (null/undefined → constant(0), number → constant, object with .sample → fromDistribution) and makes it easy to plug in new inputs and edge cases. B’s variable() only handles a function or a constant value, so new requirements (e.g. distribution objects, API-backed samplers) more often force changes to existing code. For adding many domain-specific models with different inputs, A’s variable abstraction and structure reduce how often existing code must be modified and make the design more resilient to new requirements and edge cases.

B has less incidental complexity. (1) Lines: B uses fewer source lines (~145 vs ~154) and a simpler layout (single variable abstraction, flatter stats). (2) Abstractions: A introduces three variable constructors—constant(), sampled(), arbitrary()—where constant and arbitrary are identical (both return a fixed value), so one exists only as a duplicate. sampled() exists only to wrap distribution functions into the { sample() } interface. B uses a single variable(samplerOrValue) that covers constants and samplers, and model() does one small normalization (v.sample ? v : { sample: () => v }), so there is no extra adapter layer. (3) Cognitive load: In A you must choose among three variable types (two redundant) and remember to wrap every distribution in sampled(). In B there is one way to build a variable and one model; statistics are built from small primitives (mean, median, stdDev) composed by summarize(), which matches the domain instead of a single monolithic computeStats(). (4) Constructs that exist only to support others: In A, arbitrary() and the constant/sampled/arbitrary split add no behavior, and sampled() only adapts distribution return values. In B, mean/median/stdDev are used by summarize() by design, not incidental support. Therefore B wins on less code, fewer and non-redundant abstractions, and lower cognitive load.

A has less incidental complexity. **Lines and abstractions:** A is ~159 LOC across 6 source files; B is ~188 LOC across 7 (including a barrel). A uses a single variable shape ({ sample() }) with constant, sampled, and arbitrary; distributions are plain functions wrapped by sampled(). B adds a barrel (index.js), four variable constructors (constant, fromDistribution, custom, createVariable), and distributions as { sample() } objects, so an extra adaptation layer is needed. **Cognitive load:** In A the flow is linear: build model → runSimulation → computeStats → render. B adds createVariable(spec) (polymorphic over null, number, function, object), but the app never uses it and builds variables by hand, so that concept is unused. B’s sumModel normalizes inputs with a runtime map (typeof v?.sample === 'function' ? v : { sample: () => v }), which only exists to allow non-variable values and overlaps with what createVariable could do. **Constructs that only support others:** In A, arbitrary(value) is redundant with constant(value). In B, the barrel exists only to re-export; fromDistribution exists only to adapt distribution objects to the variable interface; createVariable is unused; sumModel’s normalization exists to support mixed inputs; getElement exists only to throw on missing IDs. So A has one redundant name; B has several layers (barrel, adapter, unused factory, defensive normalization) that add incidental complexity.

B is easier to extend for multiple models (finance, project management, etc.). Extension points: A has a single monolithic computeStats and renderStats tied to one result shape—adding models that produce different metrics or domains requires modifying that pipeline and the app. B exposes composable statistics (mean, median, stdDev) and a separate summarize(), so domain-specific summaries (e.g. summarizeNPV, summarizeProject) can be added as new functions without changing existing code. createVariable(spec) in B allows data-driven variable definitions from config (numbers, functions, objects with sample), so new domains add config rather than new variable types. Central index.js in B gives one place to export new models and utilities. Modify vs add: with A, new result types or stats require editing computeStats and renderStats; with B, new models and new summary/format logic are additive. B is more resilient to new requirements (multi-metric results, percentiles, risk bands) because the design separates primitives from composed summaries and formatting.

Implementation B is easier to extend for multiple models (finance, project management) because: (1) Extension points: B exposes statistics as small composable functions (mean, median, stdDev, summarize) so domain-specific summarizers (e.g. VaR for finance, percentiles for project management) can be added without changing existing code; A's single computeStats(results) forces either modifying that function or duplicating the stats pipeline. (2) Modify vs add: With B, new models are new factories, new metrics are new functions composed into new summarizers—existing model.js, simulation.js, and statistics.js stay unchanged; with A, new domains often require changes inside computeStats or new stat modules plus changes in app.js to choose them. (3) Resilience: B's primitive stat functions handle edge cases locally and allow arbitrary return shapes per domain; A's fixed computeStats return shape and monolithic logic make new requirements and edge cases more likely to require edits to existing code. The variable layer in B (single variable(samplerOrValue)) also avoids adding new wrapper types when introducing new inputs. Both rely on a generic simulation over model.run()/evaluate(), but B's statistics design is the main differentiator for adding many calculation types.

Both implementations satisfy the task: Monte Carlo loop with iterations and model as parameters, a sum model over sampleable variables, support for distributions (normal, uniform, triangular, pareto), constants and arbitrary values, returning all results, and displaying mean, median, and ±1σ and ±2σ via vanilla HTML/DOM with tests under npm run test and no docs/comments. A is more faithful for these reasons: (1) Statistics: the spec refers to standard deviations around the mean for the simulation results; A uses the population standard deviation (divide by n), which is correct for the full set of runs; B uses sample standard deviation (n−1), which is for a sample and is a minor mismatch. (2) Tests: A’s package.json uses an explicit test path ("test/*.test.js"), so npm run test reliably runs the tests; B uses "node --test" with no path, which depends on discovery and cwd. (3) Demo and variable types: A’s demo uses all four distributions and all variable kinds (constant, sampled distributions, arbitrary), matching the spec’s “distributions … constants, or arbitrary values”; B’s UI only uses constant, uniform, and normal. (4) Spec wording: A’s variable API (constant, sampled, arbitrary) lines up directly with “constants, or arbitrary values” and distributions; B’s design (createVariable, fromDistribution, custom) is flexible but less literal. (5) Returning results: B’s summarize() helpfully returns both stats and the results array; A returns the results from runSimulation and analyzes them separately—both satisfy “all results returned for analysis,” so this is a wash. Overall A adheres more closely to the stated requirements and intent, especially on statistics and demonstrating the full variable/distribution set.

Implementation A is more faithful to the task. (1) Statistics: The task says 'all the results of the simulation should be returned allowing them to be analyzed.' That implies the full population of outcomes, so standard deviation should be population std dev. A uses population variance (divide by N) in analysis.js; B uses sample variance (N-1) in statistics.js. For analyzing the complete simulation output, A's choice is correct. (2) Explicit requirement coverage: A has a test named 'returns all results for analysis' that asserts the simulation returns the full results array; B does not test that requirement directly. (3) Variables as distributions, constants, or arbitrary: The spec lists these three cases. A exposes them as constant(), arbitrary(), and sampled(sampler), so the API matches the spec wording. B uses a single variable(samplerOrValue), which is concise but blurs constant vs arbitrary. Both support the behavior; A reflects the described categories more literally. (4) Empty results: A's computeStats returns defined values for empty results; B's summarize builds on mean/median that return NaN for empty arrays, which can propagate. (5) Both correctly take iterations and model as parameters, return the full results array, show mean/median and ±1σ/±2σ, use vanilla JS and modular reusable code, and avoid comments. Parameter order (model first vs iterations first) and method name (evaluate vs run) are unspecified and equivalent. On balance, A better matches the stated intent (population analysis, explicit 'all results' testability, and variable kinds) and the literal spec (variable types and how results are analyzed).

Implementation A (variation-b) is more faithful to the task. (1) Returning all results: The spec states 'All the results of the simulation should be returned allowing them to be analyzed.' A's summarize() returns an object that includes the full results array (s.results), so the analysis API explicitly returns all results. B's summarize() returns only the summary statistics (mean, median, bands); the raw results are not part of its return value, so 'returned for analysis' is satisfied only by keeping runSimulation()'s return value separately. A makes the full dataset part of the analysis output. (2) Variables as first-class concepts: The task describes variables that 'can be sampled' and can be 'distributions,' 'constants,' or 'arbitrary values.' A implements this with explicit variable abstractions (constant(), fromDistribution(), custom(), createVariable()) and treats distributions as objects with .sample(). B uses a single variable() wrapper and distributions as plain functions; it works but is less explicit about the three cases. (3) Both satisfy: iterations and model as parameters, sum model, distributions (normal, uniform, triangular, pareto), display of mean/median and +/- 1 and 2 standard deviations, vanilla JS/HTML, reusable modules, tests via npm run test, and no comments. A's statistics module also returns the results array from summarize(), which directly matches the requirement that all results be returned for analysis.
